items.prompt_md: In the LIFO matching approach, what do you do the moment you see an **opening** bracket?
options: Push it onto the stack
Pop the stack and discard the result
Check it against the stack's current top
Ignore it and keep scanning
items.answer: Push it onto the stack
items.explanation_md: Every opener is a new 'still unmatched' item, so it goes on top of the stack until something closes it.

items.prompt_md: The stack is empty and the current character is a **closing** bracket. What should happen?
options: Return False immediately, since there's nothing for it to match
Push the closing bracket onto the stack anyway
Skip it and continue scanning
Treat it as if it were an opening bracket
items.answer: Return False immediately, since there's nothing for it to match
items.explanation_md: A closer with nothing on the stack means it has no possible opener to pair with — the string is invalid right there.

items.prompt_md: After scanning every character, the stack still has unpopped openers sitting on it. What does that mean?
options: Some openers were never closed, so the string is invalid
The string is valid as long as no mismatches occurred mid-scan
It means the string had extra closers
It has no effect on the final answer
items.answer: Some openers were never closed, so the string is invalid
items.explanation_md: A non-empty stack at the end means at least one opener never got its matching closer — the check must be `not stack`, not just 'no mismatch happened'.

items.prompt_md: What is the overall time and worst-case space complexity of the one-pass stack solution?
options: O(n) time, O(n) worst-case space
O(1) time, O(1) space
O(n log n) time, O(n) space
O(n^2) time, O(1) space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(n) worst-case space
items.explanation_md: Each character triggers exactly one push or one pop, so the pass is linear; a string of all openers fills the stack to size n.

items.prompt_md: A buggy implementation calls `stack.pop()` before checking whether the stack is empty. What happens on an input like `']'` alone?
options: It raises an error trying to pop from an empty stack
It correctly returns False
It treats the closer as an opener
It silently returns True
items.answer: It raises an error trying to pop from an empty stack
items.explanation_md: Popping an empty stack is undefined/erroring behavior in most languages — the emptiness check must come before the pop, not after.

items.prompt_md: Why can't a queue (FIFO) replace the stack in this approach?
options: A queue would match closers against the oldest unmatched opener instead of the most recent one, breaking nested structures
A queue is asymptotically slower than a stack
A queue cannot store characters
There is no real difference — either works
items.answer: A queue would match closers against the oldest unmatched opener instead of the most recent one, breaking nested structures
items.explanation_md: Nested matching needs 'most recently opened closes first' — that's LIFO by definition, which only a stack gives you.

items.prompt_md: What cheap O(1) check, done before even scanning the string, can immediately rule out validity for some inputs?
options: If the string's length is odd, it can never be balanced, since every opener needs exactly one matching closer
If the string is non-empty
If the first character happens to be a letter
If the string contains exactly one bracket type
items.answer: If the string's length is odd, it can never be balanced, since every opener needs exactly one matching closer
items.explanation_md: Every valid nesting pairs one opener with one closer, so a valid string must have even length — an odd-length string can be rejected without scanning at all.

items.prompt_md: Why does the check need to compare `stack.pop() != pairs[ch]`, rather than only checking that the stack is non-empty?
options: Because bracket types must match too — an empty-stack check alone would wrongly accept mismatched types like "(]"
Because it makes the loop run faster
Because the language requires an equality check syntactically
It doesn't matter — either check alone is sufficient
items.answer: Because bracket types must match too — an empty-stack check alone would wrongly accept mismatched types like "(]"
items.explanation_md: Non-emptiness only guarantees *something* is there to pop — it says nothing about whether that something is the *correct* opener for this closer.

items.prompt_md: This idea generalizes beyond `()[]{}` to nested XML tags, nested function calls, or nested comments. What property must those tokens share with brackets for the same stack trick to apply?
options: Each 'open' token has exactly one well-defined 'close' counterpart, and closes must nest LIFO
The tokens must all be single characters
There must be no more than three token types
The tokens must appear an even number of times total
items.answer: Each 'open' token has exactly one well-defined 'close' counterpart, and closes must nest LIFO
items.explanation_md: The mechanism doesn't care what the tokens look like — only that 'most recently opened, first closed' is the rule governing them.

items.prompt_md: On a worst-case input like `"(((((("` (all openers), how large does the stack grow relative to the input length n?
options: O(n) — it can hold every character pushed so far
O(1) — stacks are bounded regardless of input
O(log n)
O(sqrt(n))
items.answer: O(n) — it can hold every character pushed so far
items.explanation_md: If nothing ever gets popped, every pushed character stays on the stack, so its size scales linearly with the input.

items.prompt_md: Why is popping the stack's top an O(1) operation rather than O(n)?
options: A stack only ever touches its last element, with no shifting of the other elements
Because the language secretly keeps the stack sorted
Because the stack is always small in practice
Because popping first searches for the target value
items.answer: A stack only ever touches its last element, with no shifting of the other elements
items.explanation_md: Removing from one fixed end (the top) never requires moving any other element, which is exactly what makes push/pop O(1).

items.prompt_md: The string `"([)]"` has two opens and two closes of matching types, yet it's invalid. Why?
options: The closers appear out of LIFO order — `)` tries to close `(` while `[` is still on top of the stack, unmatched
It has an odd length
It uses too many distinct bracket types
Those two bracket types can never appear in the same string
items.answer: The closers appear out of LIFO order — `)` tries to close `(` while `[` is still on top of the stack, unmatched
items.explanation_md: Having the right counts of each type isn't enough — the order in which they close has to respect 'most recently opened closes first', which this string violates.

items.prompt_md: You're given a string of parentheses, brackets, and braces and need to confirm every one closes in the correct nested order.
options: Push openers, and on each closer pop and check it matches the top
Count each bracket type and compare totals
Sort the string and check adjacent pairs
Use two pointers from both ends inward
items.answer: Push openers, and on each closer pop and check it matches the top
items.explanation_md: Correct *nesting order* — not just matching totals — is the LIFO signal: the most recently opened bracket must close first.

items.prompt_md: You need to repeatedly delete adjacent matching pairs of the same character from a string until no more pairs remain (e.g. "abba" collapses to "").
options: Push characters onto a stack, popping whenever the new character matches the top
Sort the string and remove duplicates
Count frequencies and remove characters with even counts
Use a sliding window of size 2
items.answer: Push characters onto a stack, popping whenever the new character matches the top
items.explanation_md: 'The most recent unresolved character might get cancelled by what comes next' is exactly the LIFO cancellation pattern a stack was built for.

items.prompt_md:
A document format uses opening and closing tags (like `
` ... `
`) that can nest arbitrarily deep, and you need to confirm every tag is properly closed.
options: Push tag names on open, pop and compare on close
Count how many times each tag name appears
Use a hash set of tag names that have been seen
Binary search for the matching close tag
items.answer: Push tag names on open, pop and compare on close
items.explanation_md: Tag nesting is bracket matching with words instead of symbols — the same 'most recently opened must close first' rule applies.

items.prompt_md: You're simplifying a Unix-style file path where `..` means 'go up one directory', and you need to resolve it to the final canonical path.
options: Push directory names, and pop the top whenever `..` is seen
Sort the path segments alphabetically
Count how many times each segment repeats
Use two pointers scanning from both ends
items.answer: Push directory names, and pop the top whenever `..` is seen
items.explanation_md: 'Go up one level' means undo the most recently entered directory — exactly what popping a stack's top does.

items.prompt_md: Given two sequences of push and pop operations, you need to determine whether the pop sequence is actually achievable from the push sequence on a real stack.
options: Simulate a stack: push in order, greedily popping whenever the next expected pop value is on top
Sort both sequences and compare them
Count how often each value appears in both sequences
Use two pointers, one per sequence
items.answer: Simulate a stack: push in order, greedily popping whenever the next expected pop value is on top
items.explanation_md: The question is literally about stack *behavior*, so the direct check is to simulate a stack and see if the claimed pop order can ever be produced.

items.prompt_md: You need to decode a compressed string like `2[a3[b]]` into its fully expanded form, where a number tells you how many times the following bracketed group repeats.
options: Use a stack to hold the in-progress string and repeat count each time a `[` is opened, popping and expanding on each `]`
Use a frequency map of characters
Sort the characters by how often they appear
Scan with two pointers from both ends
items.answer: Use a stack to hold the in-progress string and repeat count each time a `[` is opened, popping and expanding on each `]`
items.explanation_md: Nested `[...]` groups are unresolved work waiting to be finished innermost-first — a stack holds each unfinished outer context until its inner group is done.

items.prompt_md: For every opening bracket in an expression, you need to instantly know how deeply nested it is as you scan left to right.
options: Track nesting depth with a stack (or its current size) as you push and pop
Count total brackets and divide by two
Sort brackets by their position in the string
Use a hash map of bracket positions
items.answer: Track nesting depth with a stack (or its current size) as you push and pop
items.explanation_md: Nesting depth at any moment is just 'how many openers are currently unmatched' — the stack's size, live, as you scan.

items.prompt_md: You're checking whether a sequence of function-call log lines (`enter`/`exit`) is well-formed — every `exit` must match the most recently unmatched `enter`.
options: Push on each `enter`, pop and compare the function name on each `exit`
Count how many `enter` and `exit` lines exist and compare the totals
Sort the log lines by timestamp
Use a hash map counting calls per function name
items.answer: Push on each `enter`, pop and compare the function name on each `exit`
items.explanation_md: 'The most recently unmatched enter must be what this exit closes' is the LIFO rule stated outright — this is bracket matching wearing a different costume.

items.prompt_md: For every element in an array, you need to find the nearest element to its right that is strictly greater — and the array can be up to 10^5 elements, so rescanning forward from every index is too slow.
options: Push indices onto a stack, popping whenever a bigger value arrives
Count how many times each value appears
Sort the array and binary search
Compare every pair of elements directly
items.answer: Push indices onto a stack, popping whenever a bigger value arrives
items.explanation_md: 'Next greater element, and brute force is O(n^2)' is the monotonic-stack signal — each index waits on the stack until something bigger resolves it.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings made only of bracket characters and need to know whether they contain the same number of each bracket type, regardless of order or nesting.
options: Tally counts of each bracket type in a hash map and compare
Push openers and pop on closers to check nesting order
Use a monotonic stack of bracket counts
Scan with two pointers moving inward
items.answer: Tally counts of each bracket type in a hash map and compare
items.explanation_md: Only totals matter here, not order or nesting, so this is a counting question — no stack needed, just a frequency comparison.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of integers, you need to find two numbers that add up to a target value.
options: Hash map of value to index, checking for the complement as you scan
Push values onto a stack and pop whenever a match is found
Use a monotonic stack to track candidates
Simulate a stack of push/pop operations
items.answer: Hash map of value to index, checking for the complement as you scan
items.explanation_md: Two-sum is a direct lookup question — 'has the complement been seen' — with no LIFO or nesting relationship, so a hash map is the tool, not a stack.

items.prompt_md: Given an array, you need to determine whether it contains any value that appears more than once, with no concern for order.
options: Tally counts in a hash map (or hash set of values seen so far)
Push values onto a stack and pop when a duplicate is found
Use a monotonic stack to track the smallest unmatched value
Push and pop values to simulate matching
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map (or hash set of values seen so far)
items.explanation_md: 'Any duplicates, order irrelevant' is answered by tracking which values have been seen — a hash set membership check, not a stack.
